Discussion
In this study, AP-PLGA-NPs were successfully prepared
using a solvent diffusion methodology. In the polymeriza-
tion, the non-ionic surfactant of polysorbate 80 was used as
solubilizer, emulsiﬁer and stabilizer.[33,34] The prepared
AP-PLGA-NPs were ﬁnally coated with polysorbate 80, as it
could lead to a smaller size of NPs[35] and inﬂuence the dis-
tributions of the entrapped drugs.[25]
Particle size plays a key role for brain-targeting because it
can affect the endocytosis by the brain capillary endothelial
cells (BCECs). It is reported that NPs less than 200 nm are
more easily transported across the BBB and are less likely to

column1: Sham IR AP AP-PLGA-NPs

column2: be ﬁltered by the RES,[25,29,36] and smaller particles with
hydrophilic surfaces have prolonged circulation in the
blood,[37] while NPs with sizes above 300 nm are easily cap-
tured by Kupffer cells or other phagocytic cells which
greatly limit their biodistribution. Thus, the size
(145.0 ± 1.3 nm) of the prepared NPs was considered to be

column2: suitable for the treatment of brain diseases.
Zeta potential can inﬂuence physical stability of a colloi-
dal dispersion. High negative charge of zeta potential indi-
cates that the electrostatic repulsion between particles will
prevent the aggregation of the spheres and thus stabilize

column1: Sham IR AP AP-PLGA-NPs

column2: particle suspensions.[26] The zeta potential of AP-PLGA-NPs
was found to be −14.81 ± 1.39 mV, whereas that of empty
NPs was −36.13 ± 3.35 mV. Negative value of zeta potential
might result from the negative charge of PLGA.[38]
The release of AP from the PLGA-NPs showed biphasic
release proﬁle. In general, drug release rate depends mainly
on solubility, diffusion and biodegradation of the matrix

also affects drug release. The DL capacity is directly propor-
tional to the burst and release rate of the encapsulated drug,
and smaller particles more easily result in an initial burst
release.[17] In this study, the initial rapid release should
mainly be attributed to the weakly bound or adsorbed drug
